content: If the coefficient of $x^2$ and $x^3$ in the expansion of $\left(1+8 x+b x^2\right)(1-3 x)^9$ in the power of $x$ are equal, then $b$ is
explanation:
$\begin{array}{r}\left(1+8 x+b x^2\right)(1-3 x)^9=\left(1+8 x+b x^2\right) \\ \sum_{r=0}^9{ }^9 C_r(-3 x)^9\end{array}$

Coefficient of $x^2={ }^9 C_2 \times 9-8 \times{ }^9 C_1 \times 3+b$

Coefficient of $x^3=-{ }^9 C_3 \times 27+8 \times{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_2 \times 9-b \times{ }^9 C_1 \times 3$

$\because$ Both are equal.

$$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{9 \times 8}{2} \times 9-8 \times 9 \times 3+b=-\frac{9 \times 8 \times 7}{3 \times 2} \times 27+\frac{8 \times 9 \times 8}{2} \times 9-b \times 27\\ \Rightarrow & \quad 28 b=2592-2268-324+216 \\ \Rightarrow & 28 b=2808-2592=216 \\ \Rightarrow & b=\frac{216}{28}=\frac{54}{7} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the coefficients of $x^7$ and $x^8$ in the expansion of $\left[2+\frac{x}{3}\right]^n$ are equal, then value of $n$ is
explanation:
Coefficient of $x^7={ }^n C_7(2)^{n-7}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^7$

and Coefficient of $x^8={ }^n C_8 \cdot(2)^{n-8}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^8$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { According to the question, } \\ & { }^n C_7(2)^{n-7}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^7={ }^n C_8(2)^{n-8}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^8 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{n!}{7!(n-7)!} \cdot \frac{2^{n-7}}{2^{n-8}}=\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{n!}{8!(n-8)!} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2=\frac{7!}{8!} \cdot \frac{(n-7)!}{(n-8)!} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2=\frac{1}{8} \times(n-7) \times \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow n-7=48 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad n=55 \end{aligned}$$

content: Coefficient of $x^3$ in the expansion of $\left(x^2-x+1\right)^{10}\left(x^2+1\right)^{15}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \left(x^2-x+1\right)^{10}\left(x^2+1\right)^{15} \\ & \quad=\left(1+{ }^{10} C_1\left(x^2-x\right)+{ }^{10} C_2\left(x^2-x\right)^2+\ldots\right) \\ & \quad\left(1+{ }^{15} C_1 x^2+{ }^{15} C_2\left(x^2\right)^2+\ldots\right) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We get, the coefficient of } x^3 \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & =-2 \cdot{ }^{10} C_2 \cdot{ }^{15} C_0-{ }^{10} C_1 \cdot{ }^{15} C_1-{ }^{10} C_3 \\ & =-2 \times \frac{10 \times 9}{2} \times 1-10 \times 15-\frac{10 \times 9 \times 8}{3 \times 2} \\ & =-360 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text {} \because(9)^{50}=(10-1)^{50} \\ & =\left[{ }^{50} C_0(10)^{50}-{ }^{50} C_1(10)^{49}(1)^1+{ }^{50} C_2(10)^{48}(1)^2\right. \\ & +\ldots .]+{ }^{50} C_{48}(10)^2(1)^{48}-{ }^{50} C_{49}(10)^1+{ }^{50} C_{50}(10)^0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\because$$ All the terms in the bracket are multiples of $$10^3$$, so we can write as $$1000 \lambda$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =1000 \lambda+\frac{50 \times 49}{2} \times 100-50 \times 10+1 \times 1 \\ & =1000 \lambda+12500-500+1 \end{aligned}$$

So, last 3 digits will be $$000+500-500+1=001$$

Hence, last 3 digits will be 001.

content: If $$x^n=a_0+a_1(1+x)+a_2(1+x)^2+\ldots \ldots \ldots+ a_n(1+x)^n=b_0+b_1(1-x)+b_2(1-x)^2+\ldots . .+ b_n(1-x)^n$$, then for $$n=201,\left(a_{101}, b_{101}\right)$$ is equal to
If the 2nd, 3rd and 4th terms in the expansion of $$(a+b)^n$$ be $$240,720$$ and 1080 respectively, then the value of $$(n, b, a)$$ is
explanation:
According to the question,

$$\begin{aligned} & t_2 \text { in }(a+b)^n={ }^n C_1 a^{n-1} b=240 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & t_3 \text { in }(a+b)^n={ }^n C_2 a^{n-2} b^2=720 \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & t_4 \text { in }(a+b)^n={ }^n C_3 a^{n-3} b^3=1080 \quad \text{... (iii)} \end{aligned}$$

Now, dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get

$$\frac{n a^{n-1} b}{\frac{n(n-1)}{2} a^{n-2} b^2}=\frac{240}{720}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 a}{(n-1) b}=\frac{1}{3} \quad \text{.... (iv)}$$

Also dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (iii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\frac{n(n-1)}{2} a^{n-2} b^2}{\frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6} a^{n-3} b^3}=\frac{720}{1080} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{3 a}{(n-2) b}=\frac{2}{3} \quad \text{.... (v)} \end{aligned}$$

On dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (v), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\frac{2 a}{(n-1) b}}{\frac{3 a}{(n-2) b}}=\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{2}{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2(n-2)}{3(n-1)}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 n-8=3 n-3 \Rightarrow n=5 \\ \end{aligned}$$

From Eq. (iv)

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 a=\frac{4 b}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{2 b}{3} \Rightarrow \quad b=\frac{3 a}{2} \end{aligned}$$

From Eq. (i),

$$\begin{array}{ll} & { }^5 C_1 a^4 b=240 \\ \Rightarrow & 5 \times a^4 \times \frac{3 a}{2}=240 \\ \Rightarrow & a^5=32=2^5 \\ \Rightarrow & a=2 \Rightarrow b=\frac{3 a}{2}=3 \end{array}$$

Hence, $$(n, b, a)=(5,3,2)$$

content: The coefficient of the term independent of $$x$$ in the expansion $$\left(\frac{x+1}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x-1}{x-x^{1 / 2}}\right)^{10}$$ is
explanation:
Given expansion is

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{x+1}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x-1}{x-x^{1 / 2}}\right)^{10} \\ & =\left[\frac{\left(x^{1 / 3}\right)^3+1^3}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{\left\{(\sqrt{x})^2-1\right\}}{\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-1)}\right]^{10} \\ & =\left[\frac{\left(x^{1 / 3}+1\right)\left(x^{2 / 3}+1-x^{1 / 3}\right)}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{\left\{(\sqrt{x})^2-1\right\}}{\sqrt{x}(\sqrt{x}-1)}\right]^{10} \\ & =\left[\left(x^{1 / 3}+1\right)-\frac{(\sqrt{x}+1)}{\sqrt{x}}\right]^{10}=\left(x^{1 / 3}-x^{-1 / 2}\right)^{10} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ The general term is

$$\begin{aligned} T_{r+1} & ={ }^{10} C_r\left(x^{1 / 3}\right)^{10-r}(-x)^{-r / 2} \\ & ={ }^{10} C_r(-1)^r x^{\left(\frac{10-r}{3}-\frac{r}{2}\right)} \end{aligned}$$

For the term independent of $$x$$, put

$$\begin{array}{lc} & \frac{10-r}{3}-\frac{r}{2}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 20-2 r-3 r=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 20=5 r \Rightarrow r=4 \\ \therefore & T_5={ }^{10} C_4=\frac{10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1}=210 \end{array}$$

content: Which of the following is the correct principle of Mathematical induction?

options.content: Let $$P(n)$$ be a statement such that $n$ be any integer and $$P(1)$$ is true. Also, $$P(m)$$ is true for $$m$$, any natural number, then $$P(n)$$ is true for all integers $$n$$

Let $$P(n)$$ be a statement involving natural number $$n$$ such that $$P(1)$$ is true and $$P(m)$$ is true whenever $$P(n)$$ is true for every $$n \geq m$$, then $$P(n)$$ is true for all $$n \in N$$ (set of natural numbers)
Let $$P(n)$$ be a statement where $$n \in N$$ such that $$P(1)$$ is true and $$P(n), P(n+1)$$ also holds, then $$P(n)$$ is true $$\forall n \in N$$
Let $$P(n)$$ be a statement involving the natural number $$n$$, such that $$P(1)$$ is true and $$P(m+1)$$ is true for all $$n \leq m$$. Then, $$P(n)$$ is true for all $$n \in N$$

explanation: By first principle of mathematical induction, statement (d) is true.
